We started with a dated bathroom that wasn't doing this gorgeous arched window any justice. Our crew opened up the layout, brought in a freestanding white soaking tub, and positioned it right beneath that window where natural light just pours in. Then came the warm wood-toned floating vanity with a clean white quartz countertop, paired with matte black fixtures — the tub filler, the faucet, every detail intentional. We added a soft-edged rectangular mirror above the vanity and flanked it with industrial-style multi-light sconces that give the space so much character. The walls got a warm neutral tone, and crisp white flooring tied the whole room together into something that honestly feels like a spa you never want to leave. ✨

Our team put a lot of heart into this one, and it shows in every corner of that room.

post_context.copy:
If your bathroom fan has a humidity sensor, now's the time to double-check that setting before Toronto's summer humidity hits full force.

Most humidity-activated switches come factory-set around 60% relative humidity, which works fine in winter. But once July and August roll in, indoor humidity in GTA homes can easily sit at 65–70% without the fan ever kicking on — meaning moisture just lingers on your walls, grout, and ceiling. Drop the activation threshold down to around 50–55% so the fan actually responds when it matters. While you're at it, make sure the fan vent isn't clogged with dust — a dirty vent cuts airflow by more than you'd think. Five minutes of adjusting now can save you from peeling paint and mould patches later. 💨

If your bathroom ventilation setup needs an upgrade, we're always happy to take a look.

post_context.copy:
Before you start spring cleaning, get down and take a close look at the caulk around your toilet base.

That seal does more than keep things tidy — it's your first defence against water seeping underneath and rotting the subfloor. After a GTA winter of freeze-thaw cycles and fluctuating indoor humidity, caulk dries out, cracks, and pulls away faster than you'd think. Run your finger along the base and check for gaps, discolouration, or any sponginess in the flooring nearby. If the caulk just looks worn, scrape it out and reapply a quality kitchen-and-bath silicone — it's a 20-minute job that can save you thousands. If the floor already feels soft or you notice any odour, that's a sign the damage has gone deeper. 🔍

If things look worse than a simple recaulk can handle, we're happy to come take a look and give you an honest assessment.

PDX Remodelling Solutions - 437 238 8992
📷 Photo by Curtis Adams via Pexels

post_context.copy:
Ever had your drains slow to a crawl in January for no obvious reason? That little pipe sticking out of your roof — your plumbing vent stack — might be the culprit.

When ice and snow build up around or inside that vent, air can't flow through your drain system properly. The result? Slow drains, gurgling toilets, and sometimes sewer gas backing up into your bathroom. Before the first deep freeze hits the GTA, get up there (safely) or hire someone to check that the vent is clear of debris, leaves, or old bird nests. A simple vent cap or screen can help keep things flowing all winter. If you notice the opening looks cracked or damaged, get it sealed before freeze-thaw cycles make it worse. 🏠

If your drains are already acting up and a quick roof check doesn't solve it, we're happy to take a look and figure out what's going on.

PDX Remodelling Solutions - 437 238 8992
📷 Photo by Kindel Media via Pexels

post_context.copy:
That dark stuff creeping into your grout lines? It's about to get a whole lot worse once the windows close for winter.

Early fall is the perfect time to deep-clean your bathroom grout and seal it with a mould-resistant sealer. Start by scrubbing with a baking soda and hydrogen peroxide paste — let it sit for 10 minutes, then rinse clean. Once the grout is fully dry, apply a quality silicone-based grout sealer to lock moisture out. This matters especially here in the GTA, where our long heating season traps humidity indoors for months. A sealed grout line resists mould growth and is way easier to keep clean all winter long. 🛁

If your grout is crumbling or stained beyond saving, we're always happy to take a look and talk through your options.

post_context.copy:
When's the last time you looked under your bathroom sink? Like, really looked?

Those braided steel supply lines connected to your toilets and faucets don't last forever — most are rated for about 8–10 years. Over time the rubber lining inside degrades, and here in the GTA our winter pressure swings from freeze-thaw cycles can push a weakened hose right past its limit. A burst supply line can dump gallons of water per minute onto your floor, and it usually happens when you're not home. Take five minutes this weekend: get a flashlight, check every supply hose for bulging, rust spots, or any moisture at the fittings. If they're more than eight years old, swap them out — replacement hoses are inexpensive and a straightforward DIY job. 🔦

If you spot water damage that's already done its work, we're happy to come take a look and talk through your options.

PDX Remodelling Solutions - 437 238 8992
📷 Photo by Curtis Adams via Pexels

post_context.copy:
Ever notice those tiny slots at the bottom of your bathroom window frame? Those are weep holes — and if they're clogged, you could be in for a rough winter.

Weep holes and drainage channels let trapped moisture escape from inside the window frame. When they get blocked by dust, paint, or caulk, water sits in there with nowhere to go. Here in Toronto, that standing water freezes and expands during our freeze-thaw cycles, cracking the frame and breaking seals. Come spring, you've got water leaking into the wall and mould growing where you can't see it. Grab a toothpick or a thin wire and gently clear each weep hole out now, before temperatures start dropping. Takes about two minutes per window 🪟

If you're already seeing moisture damage or crumbling frames around your bathroom windows, we're happy to come take a look.

PDX Remodelling Solutions - 437 238 8992
📷 Photo by Curtis Adams via Pexels

post_context.copy:
If your bathroom fan sounds like it's working but your mirror still fogs up for twenty minutes — the problem might be hiding in your attic.

A lot of GTA homes have flexible dryer-style hose running from the bathroom fan to the roof vent. Over time that flex hose sags, creating low spots where condensation pools — especially during our humid summers. That trapped moisture can drip back down, encourage mould growth, and cut your airflow in half. Swapping it out for smooth rigid pipe keeps air moving efficiently and gives condensation nowhere to collect. Make sure joints slope slightly toward the exit vent so any moisture drains out instead of sitting in the line. It's a straightforward upgrade that makes a real difference 💨

If you're not comfortable working in the attic or want it done right the first time, we're always happy to help out.

PDX Remodelling Solutions - 437 238 8992
📷 Photo by Max Vakhtbovych via Pexels

post_context.copy:
Those tiny cracks in your shower floor grout? They didn't appear out of nowhere.

After a GTA winter, the freeze-thaw cycle does a number on grout lines — especially in older bathrooms where moisture seeps in and expands as it freezes. Get down close to your shower floor with a flashlight and run your finger along the grout. If you feel any crumbling, see hairline fractures, or notice discolouration, that's your warning sign. Once Toronto's humid spring and summer hit, those small openings become an open invitation for mould to settle in underneath your tiles where you can't even see it. The fix is straightforward: scrape out the damaged grout with a grout saw, clean the joints, and re-grout with a mould-resistant formula. Seal it once it cures and you're set for the season. 🔧

If the damage goes deeper than the grout or tiles are starting to lift, we're always happy to come take a look and give you an honest assessment.
